Patch Set 5:

It isn't immediately obvious to me that this is correct - I would rather the commit message made it obvious.
A signal might wake an l_wait_event() that wake_up_process() wouldn't.
If the signaller doesn't make the wait condition true, if the l_wait_event call has allowed interrupts, then the behaviour can be different.

There are two cases here: li_task and lad_task.
The wait for li_task will abort on an interrupt, and the condition includes !thread_is_running()
The wakeup of li_task sets the thread flag SVC_STOPPING before triggering the wakeup.
So the condition will become true and the fact that a signal aborted the wait isn't important.

For lad_task there are two waits in lfsck_assistant_engine()
The second will not abort on SIGINT, so the behaviour there is unchanged when switching to wake_up_process() (and the thread_is_running() test will ensure the wait aborts).

The first will abort on SIGINT, so we need to convince ourselves that the signaller makes the condition true.
The condition is
			     !lfsck_assistant_req_empty(lad) ||
			     test_bit(LAD_EXIT, &lad->lad_flags) ||
			     test_bit(LAD_TO_POST, &lad->lad_flags) ||
			     test_bit(LAD_TO_DOUBLE_SCAN, &lad->lad_flags),
I cannot see why we can be sure that this condition will be true when lfsck_stop() wakes up lad_task?  If it isn't, l_wait_event() will just go back to sleep.
Should lfsck_stop() set LAD_EXIT in lad->lad_flags ???

Patch Set 5: Code-Review-1

This wake_up_process() variant doesn't work (yet).

Patch Set 5:

I recommend changing lfsck_stop to set LAD_EXIT in lad->lad_flags before calling wake_up_process.

Patch Set 5:

So it appears that the reason for using force_sig() is that a task may be blocked waiting for rpc activity to complete.
Specifically in ptlrpc_set_wait() in 
		rc = l_wait_event(set->set_waitq,
				  ptlrpc_check_set(NULL, set), &lwi);

Backtrace of target task needing to be 'woke':
[shaun@mds ~]$ sudo cat /proc/23479/stack 
[<ffffffffc0f01330>] ptlrpc_set_wait+0x480/0x790 [ptlrpc]
[<ffffffffc0f016c3>] ptlrpc_queue_wait+0x83/0x230 [ptlrpc]
[<ffffffffc1438063>] osp_remote_sync+0xd3/0x200 [osp]
[<ffffffffc141f4fc>] osp_xattr_get+0x70c/0x1100 [osp]
[<ffffffffc119ffb9>] lfsck_read_stripe_lmv+0x2a9/0x5e0 [lfsck]
[<ffffffffc11a579b>] lfsck_namespace_handle_striped_master+0x34b/0xd28 [lfsck]
[<ffffffffc1179b6e>] lfsck_namespace_assistant_handler_p1+0xb3e/0x2090 [lfsck]
[<ffffffffc115c48f>] lfsck_assistant_engine+0x3cf/0x21d0 [lfsck]
[<ffffffffac0c50d1>] kthread+0xd1/0xe0
[<ffffffffac78cd37>] ret_from_fork_nospec_end+0x0/0x39
[<ffffffffffffffff>] 0xffffffffffffffff

So I think it's best to revert back to the first scheme?

Patch Set 5:

> I recommend changing lfsck_stop to set LAD_EXIT in lad->lad_flags before calling wake_up_process.

Yeah that mostly works. There is at least one sanity-lfsck test that doesn't like LAD_EXIT reporting 'complete' where 'stopped' state is expected.
Still it's the rpc layer "hang" that is the real issue.

Patch Set 5:

> So it appears that the reason for using force_sig() is that a task may be blocked waiting for rpc activity to complete.

I see... this is one of the problems of using signals - it is hard to work out what the exact consequence is.

Maybe it is best to go for the original solution for now, but we need a real solution eventually.
Maybe the "rq_allow_intr" and "set_allow_intr" flags could be replaced by a pointer to a bool (or similar).  If ptlrpc_set_wait() sees that pointer pointing to a non-zero value, it aborts the loop.

Plumbing that pointer all the way down might be messy though..
Maybe it could be passed down in the lu_env (but just quietly.... I dislike the whole lu_env concept and would rather see it deprecated).
Uploaded patch set 6.

Patch Set 6:

Looks good to me.

parents.subject: LU-12634 build: Recognize ELRepo -ml mainline kernel

commit.subject: LU-12634 libcfs: force_sig() removed task parameter
commit.message:
LU-12634 libcfs: force_sig() removed task parameter

Linux 5.3 removed the task parameter for force_sig()
signal: Remove task parameter from force_sig

When force_sig() is not available reset the target thread
default handler to SIG_DFL and proceed to use send_sig(..., 1)
which eventually marshals the same signal to the target task.

kernel-commit: 3cf5d076fb4d48979f382bc9452765bf8b79e740

NOTE: force_sig() is used here instead of a wake_up_process() as tasks
      may be blocked on rpc activity.

Test-Parameters: trivial
Cray-bug-id: LUS-7689
Signed-off-by: Shaun Tancheff <stancheff@cray.com>
